title: Prophylaxis
subtitle: Stop the idea before it starts
summary: Learn Nimzowitsch's habit of asking what your opponent wants before choosing your own move.
attribution: Original prose; ideas after Nimzowitsch, My System. No book text reproduced.

steps.title: Ask their question first
steps.markdown: [[prophylaxis]] means spotting the opponent's idea early and making it fail. Before you chase your own plan, ask: what would they play if they got another free move?

steps.title: Meet the threat
steps.markdown: White is aiming queen and bishop at f7. Black should develop while blocking that line. Find the calm defensive move.

hints.text: Use a developing move, not a pawn panic.
Put a knight between the queen and f7.

feedback.correct: Right. Nf6 develops a piece and takes the direct shot at f7 off the board.
feedback.wrong: Look for a move that develops and interrupts the queen's path to f7.

steps.title: The prophylactic habit
steps.markdown: What is the useful question to ask before every move?

options.label: What is my opponent threatening?
options.explain: Yes. Once you see their idea, you can stop it, ignore it safely, or make a stronger threat.

options.label: Can I make the prettiest move?
options.explain: Pretty moves are useful only when they answer the position's real demands.

options.label: Can I trade anything?
options.explain: Trades can help, but prophylaxis starts by reading the opponent's plan.

steps.title: Quiet moves count
steps.markdown: A prophylactic move is not passive. It is a move that removes the opponent's best idea while improving your own position. Strong players win many games by making danger disappear before it becomes visible.
